title: Maharashtrian Puran Poli

ingredients.name: chana dal (split Bengal gram), for puran
jaggery, grated or broken
visual_checks: the quantity of jaggery should equal the cooked, drained chana dal in volume
ingredients.notes: the quantity of jaggery should equal the cooked, drained chana dal in volume

subs.name: equal weight brown sugar or coconut sugar; the flavour will be less complex
half maida (plain flour) and half atta gives a more supple dough that is easier for beginners; the poli will be slightly lighter in colour

ingredients.name: turmeric powder (small pinch for dough)
visual_checks: gives the poli its traditional pale yellow hue
ingredients.notes: gives the poli its traditional pale yellow hue

ingredients.name: ghee, for frying and serving
visual_checks: puran poli served without ghee is considered incomplete in Maharashtra
ingredients.notes: puran poli served without ghee is considered incomplete in Maharashtra

subs.name: good-quality white butter for a less rich version; oil is not recommended as it lacks the nutty flavour that finishes the dish

steps.text: Rinse chana dal and soak for 30 minutes. Pressure-cook with 2 cups water and a pinch of turmeric for 4 whistles on medium until soft. Drain all water completely using a colander — the dal must be as dry as possible; any moisture will make the filling watery and difficult to stuff.

Cook the drained chana dal in a thick pan with jaggery on medium heat, stirring constantly, until the jaggery melts and the mixture dries into a cohesive ball that leaves the sides of the pan clean — about 10–12 minutes. This mixture is the puran. Add cardamom powder and nutmeg, stir and cool completely. Pass through a puran poli press or a coarse sieve, or mash very finely until smooth.

Make a soft, pliable dough with wheat flour, oil, turmeric, salt and enough water. The dough must be notably softer than roti dough — almost sticky. Coat it with 1 tbsp oil, cover with a damp cloth and rest for 30 minutes. A soft dough is the key to thin, un-torn polis.

Divide the dough into 12 balls and the puran into 12 equal portions. Flatten a dough ball on an oiled surface (not floured), place a puran ball in the centre and fold the dough around it, sealing firmly. Flatten gently with your palm, then roll out to a thin 15–16 cm circle using light, even pressure. Roll from the centre outward; never press down on the edges.

Heat a tawa on medium. Cook each poli for 45 seconds, flip when dry spots appear, and cook the second side for 45 seconds. Spread 0.5 tsp ghee on the top face and flip; cook for 20 seconds until golden patches form. Spread more ghee on the second face, flip and cook for 10 seconds. The total tawa time per poli is about 2 minutes.

Stack the polis and serve warm, each one topped with an extra drizzle of ghee. Puran poli is traditionally eaten with amti dal poured over the top (the combination is called varan-bhat puran poli) or with a bowl of warm milk spiked with cardamom and saffron.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: jaggery → equal weight brown sugar or coconut sugar; the flavour will be less complex; wheat-flour → half maida (plain flour) and half atta gives a more supple dough that is easier for beginners; the poli will be slightly lighter in colour; ghee → good-quality white butter for a less rich version; oil is not recommended as it lacks the nutty flavour that finishes the dish
